lines.text: Against Me!, 'Sink, Florida, Sink.' 2007. New Wave. Laura Jane Grace founded this band in Naples, moved to Gainesville, and built something that matters.
This song is about leaving. About watching a place you know drown under its own weight. I've been thinking about that all night. About what we're watching happen on the West Side right now.

Grace came out as transgender in 2012. Built a studio in Florida. Transgender Dysphoria Blues came out independent in 2014 because she had to own her own truth. That album could've broken her. Instead it broke open something in all of us.
James Bowman's been on guitar since 2001. That's loyalty. That's punk rock. Watching the West Side collapse and staying at the mic anyway—that's what this band taught us.
Bouncing Souls, 'Ole.' 1999. Hopeless Romantic. New Brunswick, New Jersey. Four guys from Basking Ridge who decided the world needed them.
Pete Steinkopf. Bryan Kienlen. Greg Attonito. They got the band's name from Doc Martens advertising—'with Bouncing Soles.' That's punk rock. That's beautiful.

They built Chunksaah Records because no one else would release them. They called their fans 'True Believers.' There's a song called 'Gone' written when one of them was homeless, standing on a street corner hearing a song in a parked car that gave him hope. He wanted to give that to someone else.

I'm thinking about that. About hope on a street corner. About 'Ole'—that rally cry that means *we're still here*. Sing it with me, Madison. Sing it in your barricaded kitchen. {{CLOCK}}. We're still here.

tracks.title: Sunday Morning
tracks.artist: Me First and the Gimme Gimmes

lines.text: Me First and the Gimme Gimmes, 'Sunday Morning.' 1997. San Francisco cover band. Fat Mike of NOFX. Joey Cape. These guys came together to play covers during off-time from their real bands.
They didn't mean to be a full-time thing. Recorded at One Week Records in San Francisco, Ryan Greene producing. Just some friends playing other people's songs better than the originals sometimes.

There's a story about them playing a Bar Mitzvah at Jonny's place. Thirty people. They made mistakes. Started a song, stopped, started again. The kid's mom was probably horrified. That's the Gimmes—punk rock as it should be. Imperfect. Present. Real.
It's {{CLOCK}}. Three hours until the sun hits Lake Mendota. You know what Sunday morning feels like? It feels like survival.

tracks.title: A Better Place, A Better Time

lines.text: Hot Water Music, 'A Better Place, A Better Time.' 1997. Fuel for the Hate Game. Gainesville, Florida. Four guys who moved there because one of them—Jason Black—said the music scene was worth the risk.

They named the band after Charles Bukowski's short story collection. They were frustrated. No opportunities back home. So they packed up and built something in Gainesville that outlasted most bands in this century.

Chuck Ragan and Chris Wollard do dual vocals on every track. Call and response. One shouts, the other answers. That's the whole thing right there. You're not alone in this. Someone's on the other end of the line.

They broke up in '98 because touring was killing them. Personal relationships were bleeding out. Then they had a farewell show in Gainesville and during rehearsal for that final gig, they wrote four new songs and realized they didn't want to die. They came back. That's punk rock. That's survival.

The Lawrence Arms, 'Coming Home.' 2004. The Greatest Story Ever Told. Chicago. Brendan Kelly and Chris McCaughan lived in an apartment complex called the Lawrence Arms before they got evicted in the middle of the night. So they named their band after their eviction.

That first album was A Guided Tour of Chicago—released before they'd even played a single show. Released it on Asian Man Records because they had a relationship there. These guys wrote about growing up in Chicago like it was scripture.

Brendan Kelly wrote songs about alcohol. About experience. About the things that break you and the things you do to survive breaking. He said, 'We all have good experiences and terrible ones influenced by alcohol. That's what we write about.'

The Lawrence Arms got booted from Warped Tour in 2002 for calling out five-dollar bottled water. Called the whole thing a dismantling of everything punk rock had built. That's politics. That's integrity. That's coming home knowing you fought something that mattered.

Alkaline Trio, 'Stupid Kid.' 2000. Maybe I'll Catch Fire. Chicago. Matt Skiba, Dan Andriano, Glenn Porter. Three guys writing about depression, alcoholism, death, broken hearts—all the things worth writing about.

Recorded at Atlas Studios in Chicago with Matt Allison. No major label polish. Just three kids saying the things nobody wants to hear out loud at parties. 'Stupid Kid'—that's every one of us at 4am thinking we figured it all out.

The band's part of Chicago's punk circle. Skiba was in Blunt and Traitors before this. Andriano played in Slapstick. These are guys who came up together. Who watched each other become themselves through music.

Derek Grant replaced Glenn Porter. Played for The Suicide Machines. All these bands feeding each other, touring together, building something that lasted because it was built on real relationships. Not industry. Not calculation. Just friends screaming into the dark.
I'm a stupid kid too. We all are. Out here at {{CLOCK}} on a night when the West Side's burning and we're still playing records. That's what stupid kids do. We keep going.

NOFX, 'Lincoln Barrett Lotus Wonton Intercourse.' 1999. The Decline era. Fat Mike, Erik Sandin, Eric Melvin, El Hefe. Los Angeles, 1983. Some kids from Fairfax High School who decided punk rock needed to be stupid and smart at the same time.

They named the band after Negative FX from Boston. They never signed to a major label despite selling gold. Built Fat Wreck Chords because they had to control their own story. Because the machine was trying to swallow them and they said no.

The Decline is eighteen minutes of fury. One track. 1999. Social commentary so cynical and fiery it became scripture. Fat Mike released a solo show as Cokie the Clown at SXSW in 2010 and debuted a song called 'Drinking Pee.' That's the commitment to the bit. That's NOFX.

They made 'Leave It Alone' for MTV and then didn't send it. Said, 'We don't want to be part of that machine.' That's integrity. That's what four decades of punk rock looks like when you don't sell the thing that's supposed to sell you.

This song's title is basically a joke. It's supposed to be. It's supposed to be ridiculous and clever and dumb all at once because that's what happens when you're awake and angry at {{CLOCK}} on a night like this. You laugh or you break.

tickerItems.text: West Side (Monroe St / Nakoma) kill count up 18% this hour vs 2168. Hilldale corridor seeing concentrated activity. Mendota shoreline quiet.
PurgeShield West Side Installation — we can reinforce your perimeter before dawn. Call 608-SHIELD-1. This is Nick. I'm recommending this.
State Street ruins quiet last two hours. Capitol Square holding. Beltline corridor: no new major incidents since 01:20. Stay home. Sunrise at 07:15.
